title: 48-Team World Cup Field Complete — All Six Finals Decided on March 31
detail: The 48-team field for the 2026 FIFA World Cup was fully confirmed on March 31 as all six qualification finals were played. The final six berths went to Bosnia-Herzegovina (Path A — beat Italy on penalties), Sweden (Path B — 3–0 vs Poland), Kosovo (Path C — 4–1 vs Turkey), Denmark (Path D — beat Czechia), DR Congo (Intercontinental Pathway 1 — beat Jamaica 1-0 AET via Axel Tuanzebe's extra-time goal), and Iraq (Intercontinental Pathway 2 — beat Bolivia 2-1 in Monterrey). Historic moments: Italy missed the World Cup for a third consecutive time; Kosovo qualified for their first-ever World Cup; DR Congo qualified for the first time in 52 years; Iraq qualified despite extraordinary travel disruptions due to the regional conflict. FIFA confirmed the last-minute ticket sales phase opens April 1 at 11:00 AM ET on a first-come, first-served basis. The 48-nation tournament begins June 11 at Estadio Azteca with Mexico vs South Africa, with the Final at MetLife Stadium, New Jersey on July 19.

title: Bosnia Knock Out Italy on Penalties — Azzurri Miss Third Straight World Cup
detail: Bosnia-Herzegovina eliminated Italy 1–1 (a.e.t.), winning 4–2 on penalties in Zenica's Stadion Bilino Polje to book a place in Group B at the 2026 World Cup alongside Canada, Qatar, and Switzerland. It is Italy's third consecutive failure to qualify — joining their 2018 exit to Sweden and 2022 exit to North Macedonia in what is now the darkest period in Azzurri history. Bosnia's Edin Džeko (40 years old) was instrumental throughout. Italy midfielder Manuel Locatelli: 'We know full well that we are responsible for all those kids out there and the entire Italian football movement.' Matteo Politano (32): 'For me, and for a few of the other senior players, it's probably our last chance.' Italy Sports Minister Andrea Abodi: 'For generations of Italians, the World Cup was the time when the country came together and waved our flag.' Coach Gennaro Gattuso, who took over from Spalletti after Italy's worst qualifying campaign in decades, now faces an overhaul of the national program.

media.caption: Bosnia knock out Italy on penalties — Azzurri miss third straight World Cup

title: Kosovo Qualify for First-Ever World Cup — Historic 4–1 Win Over Turkey in Pristina
detail: Kosovo made football history on March 31, qualifying for their first-ever FIFA World Cup with a 4–1 victory over Turkey (Türkiye) at Stadiumi Fadil Vokrri in Pristina. Kosovo — a nation that joined FIFA only in May 2016 — will compete in Group D alongside the United States, Paraguay, and Australia. The result ends Turkey's bid to end a 24-year World Cup absence. Kosovo's coach Franco Foda said before the match: 'We are in euphoria right now but we need to play the last game with calm. We have a stadium only for 13,000 spectators, but if it were possible, there would be 100,000.' The Kosovo government had promised players a €1 million bonus for qualifying. Kosovo's triumph followed their stunning 4–3 upset of Slovakia in the semi-final. The Group D draw means the USMNT will face Kosovo — the newest nation ever to qualify for a World Cup — in their group stage.

title: Sweden 3–0 Poland — Gyökeres Seals World Cup Berth; Lewandowski's Dream Ends
detail: Sweden defeated Poland 3–0 at Strawberry Arena (Friends Arena) in Solna to qualify for the 2026 FIFA World Cup, entering Group F alongside the Netherlands, Japan, and Tunisia. Arsenal striker Viktor Gyökeres — who scored a hat-trick against Ukraine in the semi-final — was the decisive force throughout Sweden's playoff campaign, establishing himself as one of Europe's most feared forwards. Poland captain Robert Lewandowski (age 37), competing in what was almost certainly his last opportunity to play at a World Cup, was kept scoreless throughout the playoff round. Ukraine coach Serhii Rebrov had said of Gyökeres: 'He played great and made the difference tonight. He showed his quality, why he plays for Arsenal.' Swedish media dubbed him 'Ibrahimovic-superior' across the campaign.

title: Denmark Qualify for World Cup — Beat Czechia in Prague to Join Group A
detail: Denmark secured qualification for the 2026 FIFA World Cup with a victory over the Czech Republic in Prague's EPET ARENA (Path D Final), joining Group A alongside Mexico, South Africa, and South Korea. Denmark were heavy favorites, having thrashed North Macedonia 4–0 in the semi-final. The Czech Republic — who had survived a dramatic 4–2 penalty shootout against Ireland in their semi — could not overcome Denmark's quality across the full 90 minutes. Denmark's World Cup return adds a significant European presence to Group A, raising the competition stakes for Mexico's opening match at Estadio Azteca on June 11.

title: DR Congo Qualify for World Cup — First Time in 52 Years After Extra-Time Win Over Jamaica
detail: DR Congo qualified for the 2026 FIFA World Cup for the first time since 1974 — a 52-year absence — defeating Jamaica 1–0 after extra time in the FIFA Intercontinental Playoff Pathway 1 Final at Estadio Akron in Guadalajara. Axel Tuanzebe's extra-time goal decided the match. DR Congo, who had earned their intercontinental playoff spot by winning a CAF playoff tournament ahead of Nigeria, Cameroon, and Gabon, enter Group K alongside Portugal, Colombia, and Uzbekistan. Jamaica's Reggae Boyz — who had beaten New Caledonia 1–0 in the semifinal — fell short of ending their own 28-year World Cup absence (Jamaica last played at France 1998). DR Congo's emotional qualification was celebrated as a landmark moment for Central African football.

media.caption: DR Congo celebrate World Cup qualification — first time in 52 years — after beating Jamaica in Guadalajara

title: Iraq Qualify for World Cup — Beat Bolivia 2–1 in Monterrey Despite Travel Ordeal
detail: Iraq qualified for the 2026 FIFA World Cup with a 2–1 victory over Bolivia in the FIFA Intercontinental Playoff Pathway 2 Final at Estadio BBVA in Monterrey, earning a place in Group I alongside France, Senegal, and Norway. Iraq's preparations were severely disrupted by ongoing regional conflict — the squad traveled overland via Jordan then flew via an extraordinary route to reach Monterrey, an odyssey that became a global story of football perseverance. Bolivia, who had been the seventh-place CONMEBOL qualifier and beaten Suriname 2-1 in the semifinal, was bidding to return to the World Cup for the first time since 1994. Iraq's result means the Lions of Mesopotamia — who last played a World Cup in 1986 — now enter Group I for a historic clash with France. Bolivia is eliminated.

media.caption: Iraq players celebrate their extraordinary World Cup qualification in Monterrey after beating Bolivia 2-1

title: USMNT 0–2 Portugal — Second Pre-Tournament Defeat Compounds World Cup Alarm
detail: The United States Men's National Team suffered a second straight pre-tournament defeat, losing 0–2 to Portugal at Mercedes-Benz Stadium in Atlanta (attendance 72,297). Francisco Trincão scored in the 37th minute and João Félix added a second in the 59th minute. The US had more shots in the first half (8 vs 3) but was undone by defensive errors on both goals. Goalkeeper Matt Freese made multiple saves and was not blamed for either goal. The result, combined with the 2–5 loss to Belgium on March 28, sparked intense discussion about Pochettino's defensive setup with the World Cup just 10 weeks away. Portugal played without Cristiano Ronaldo, Rafael Leão, and Diogo Costa. Pochettino said the results gave him 'clear information' on what needs to be fixed. The USMNT's next game is a May 31 friendly vs. Senegal in Charlotte; Pochettino's 26-player World Cup roster is announced May 26.

media.caption: USMNT fall 0-2 to Portugal at Mercedes-Benz Stadium in the final pre-tournament camp friendly
